text: The surgery begins with the elevation of the omentum to access the transverse colon and gastrocolic ligament. The gastrocolic ligament is divided along the transverse colon, allowing the omentum to be dissected from the colon's mesentery. The two-surgeon technique involves the assistant using two graspers for traction while the trainee sharply dissects the omentum from the transverse colon and mesentery using scissors. The attending surgeon assists by retracting with robotic arms one and two.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the omentum from the colonic mesentery, revealing the lesser sac by accessing the posterior aspect of the stomach. This exposure will aid in the subsequent dissection of the transverse colon mesentery.

The surgeon begins the D-III lymph node dissection by identifying and dissecting along the superior mesenteric vein, reflecting the D-III lymph nodes to the patient's right for en bloc resection. The ileocolic pedicle is identified, and the right colon mesentery is elevated off the retroperitoneum in a medial-to-lateral fashion, preserving the visceral peritoneum for complete mesocolic excision.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and head of the pancreas in relation to the superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic pedicle is re-visualized, and the ileocolic vein and artery are separated, isolated, ligated, and divided, demonstrating central vascular ligation.

The surgeon continues dissection along the superior mesenteric vein, identifying the trunk of Henle. The pancreatic branch of the trunk of Henle remains intact. The focus shifts to visualizing the middle colic artery.

The surgeon isolates, ligates, and divides the right branch of the middle colic artery. The transverse colon mesentery is divided at the site of the proposed transverse colon division, with prior dissection of the omentum aiding in the isolation and division of the middle colic pedicle.

The attending surgeon uses arms one and two to retract while the trainee utilizes arm four to dissect. The final mobilization of the right colon is achieved by dissecting the attachments at the hepatic flexure and the right white line of Toldt, medializing the right colon. The right ureter and iliac vessels are identified during this process.

The surgeon divides the ileum mesentery up to the terminal ileum, using a robotic sixty millimeter blue load stapler to staple and divide the bowel. The attending surgeon assists by retracting with robotic arms one and two, while the trainee operates arm four.

The surgeon uses an identical stapler load to staple the transverse colon. The specimen is elevated over the liver, and the remaining bowel is prepared for anastomosis. An intracorporeal isoparasotic anastomosis is created by performing aneurotomy and colonomy, with the attending surgeon assisting in approximating the ileum with the transverse colon using robotic arms one and two, while the trainee inserts and deploys the stapler through arm four.

The surgeon uses a robotic sixty-millimeter blue-glue stapler to create a common end-running collotomy, which is then closed with two layers of sutures: a running Ethabon suture followed by interrupted Beichel sutures.

The surgeon replaces the omentum and closes the twelve millimeter port site using a suture-pasture technique. The specimen is removed through an upper midline incision, with the option for extraction via a fan and steel incision. The right hemicolectomy is assessed, including a D-III lymphadenectomy.
